results.hypothesis: Applying dynamic ensemble selection techniques to time series forecasting can improve the accuracy o
results.minimum_experiment:
MECHANISM: Applying dynamic ensemble selection techniques to time series forecasting can improve the accuracy of predictions. VARIABLE: V = (ensemble_prediction - actual_value) / actual_value

INTERVENTION: Dynamic ensemble selection technique vs baseline autoregressive integrated moving average (ARIMA) model. Simulated as: generate 1000 time series data points using numpy/scipy with a mix of trends and seasonality.

MEASUREMENT: Metric: Mean Absolute Percentage Error (MAPE), computed as (1/n) * Σ|((ensemble_prediction - actual_value) / actual_value)| * 100

SUCCESS CRITERION: MAPE condition with numeric threshold: MAPE < 10%

CONTROL: Two confounders held constant - the length of the time series (n=1000) and the number of ensemble models (k=5)

results.hypothesis: Applying memory mapping techniques to transactional memory systems can improve performance.
results.minimum_experiment:
MECHANISM: Applying memory mapping techniques to transactional memory systems can reduce memory access latency, VARIABLE: V = transaction_time

INTERVENTION: We compare a transactional memory system using memory mapping (technique) against a baseline system without memory mapping. Simulated as: generate transaction_times using numpy/scipy, with technique group having a lower mean transaction time.

MEASUREMENT: Metric: average_transaction_time, computed as (sum_of_transaction_times / total_transactions)

SUCCESS CRITERION: average_transaction_time < 0.5 seconds, indicating a significant improvement in performance.

CONTROL: We hold constant the number of concurrent transactions and the size of the transactional memory to isolate the effect of memory mapping on performance.

results.hypothesis: Applying techniques from social network analysis to average-atom models can improve the accuracy of
results.minimum_experiment:
MECHANISM: Applying social network analysis techniques to average-atom models can improve simulation accuracy by capturing complex interactions between atoms. VARIABLE: V = average_atom_interaction_energy

INTERVENTION: Social network analysis technique (e.g. community detection) vs baseline (mean-field approximation). Simulated as: generate 1000 average-atom models using numpy/scipy with varying interaction energies and community structures.

MEASUREMENT: Metric: average_atom_interaction_energy, computed as (1/N) * sum(i=1 to N) (ei * xi), where ei is the interaction energy and xi is the community assignment of atom i.

SUCCESS CRITERION: average_atom_interaction_energy condition with numeric threshold: average_atom_interaction_energy > 0.8 * baseline_average_atom_interaction_energy.

CONTROL: 1-2 confounders held constant: total number of atoms (N) and average atom degree (k) are fixed across all simulations.

results.hypothesis: The introduction of probabilistic balancing in AVL trees can reduce the average rotation count per i
results.minimum_experiment:
MECHANISM: The introduction of probabilistic balancing in AVL trees reduces the average rotation count per insertion, VARIABLE: V = (rotation_count / insertion_count)

INTERVENTION: We compare the standard AVL tree insertion technique (baseline) with the probabilistic balancing technique (intervention). Simulated as: generate 1000 random insertion sequences using numpy, with keys uniformly distributed between 0 and 1000.

MEASUREMENT: Metric: average_rotation_count, computed as (total_rotation_count / total_insertion_count) = (sum(rotation_count_per_insertion) / 1000)

SUCCESS CRITERION: average_rotation_count < 0.25, indicating a significant reduction in rotation count per insertion.

CONTROL: Tree height and insertion sequence length are held constant, with tree height set to 10 and insertion sequence length set to 1000.

results.hypothesis: Applying the LZ77 compression algorithm to DNA sequences can achieve a higher compression ratio than
results.minimum_experiment:
MECHANISM: Causal mechanism — VARIABLE: V = compression_ratio = (1 - (compressed_size / original_size))

INTERVENTION: Technique vs baseline, simulated as: generate random DNA sequences using numpy and apply LZ77 compression algorithm vs standard Huffman coding, with sequence lengths ranging from 100 to 1000.

MEASUREMENT: Metric: compression_ratio, computed as (1 - (compressed_size / original_size)), where compressed_size is the size of the compressed DNA sequence and original_size is the size of the original DNA sequence.

SUCCESS CRITERION: compression_ratio > 0.7, indicating a higher compression ratio than standard methods.

CONTROL: Sequence composition (e.g., GC-content) and sequence length distribution are held constant across both techniques.
